problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem A (continuous): build a mathematical model for fungal decomposition of woody fibers and ground litter that (1) couples each fungus's hyphal extension rate and moisture tolerance, (2) captures interactions among multiple fungal species with different growth rates and moisture tolerances, (3) characterizes short- vs long-term dynamics and sensitivity to rapid/atmospheric environmental change, (4) predicts dominant/inferior species and persistent combinations across arid, semi-arid, temperate, arboreal, and tropical-rainforest environments, and (5) assesses how fungal biodiversity affects overall decomposition efficiency — plus a 2-page popular-science article. This paper's thesis: derive a closed decomposition-rate law from extension rate + moisture trade-off (parameters fit by SGD), then drive a 500x500 cellular automaton whose local rules encode that law to simulate growth, decomposition, competition, environment, and diversity.
models_used: Extension-rate model via an Arrhenius-type relation: V_E = V_Emax * exp(-V_Emax/(T*H)) with a mirrored branch for T>Tmax (piecewise, zero outside [0,2*Tmax]), making V_E a unimodal function of temperature peaking at Tmax and increasing in humidity

Closed-form decomposition-rate law V_D = T * V_E^(1/2) + lambda*a^M + K, combining a half-power (1/2-order) dependence on extension rate with an exponential dependence on the moisture trade-off M (derived from a linear log V_D vs M relation, exponentiated so lambda=10^V_D0, a=10^alpha)
Moisture trade-off mapped linearly from each fungus's lower humidity limit: M = -2*H_l + 1 (range [-1,1])

Nonlinear least-squares parameter-estimation model fitting {lambda,a,K} to literature decomposition data (Lustenhouwer et al. PNAS 2020, ref [5]) by minimizing sum of squared residuals; solved with Stochastic Gradient Descent (per-sample analytic gradients for lambda, a, K; converged to lambda=31.90, a=1.26, K=-64.80)

Cellular automaton on a 500x500 grid G for the single-fungus / multi-species breakdown process: each cell is uninfected wood or claimed by species i; probabilistic infection of neighboring cells plus a finite decomposition time per cell, with decomposed cells optionally reset to simulate falling leaves

Extension probability rule P_E = sigmoid(1/V_E) converting a continuous rate into a per-step neighbor-infection probability (Definition 1); decomposition time T_D = c*122/(V_D*500^2) converting decomposition rate into the dwell time to fully break down a cell (Definition 2, c=5e6)

Competition model via a competitive-extension probability P_C = sigmoid(1/(V_E*(1 - sum_j|N_j|/500^2))) (Definition 3) that throttles infection probability by global fungal occupancy, rewriting the CA to model interspecific competition for nutrients (mutual benefit excluded by argument)
key_techniques: Two-layer architecture: an analytic 'rate law' layer (Arrhenius + half-power + exponential moisture term) feeds a discrete simulation layer (cellular automaton), so the continuous biology and the spatial dynamics are cleanly separated yet coupled through V_E and V_D

Borrowing trait data from a single authoritative source (Lustenhouwer et al., PNAS 2020) for 6 named fungi (Trichoderma, Penicillium, Aspergillus, Rust fungi, Gibberella, Fusarium) with concrete Tmax, V_Emax, H_l, M values — grounding an abstract problem in real, citable parameters
Deriving analytic SGD update rules (explicit partial derivatives for lambda, a, K) and showing the loss/parameter convergence curve as evidence the fit is real, not asserted
Sigmoid 'squashing' to convert unbounded continuous rates into bounded [0,1] per-step probabilities — a clean continuous-to-discrete bridge for the CA
Encoding competition as a single global occupancy factor (1 - total fungi / grid area) inside the probability, giving an emergent, density-dependent slowdown without modeling pairwise interactions

Systematic experiment matrix: same CA reused across 5 real climate regions (with NOAA-sourced T/H and lat/long) and multiple species combinations to answer the dominant/inferior-species and persistence questions

Quantified, falsifiable headline numbers throughout (decomposition rate 0.68 vs 0.39 with competition = 42.69% lower; 33.65% fewer fungi under competition; 8% T/H change flips dominant species; multi-species decomposition 2.89x single-species over 1000 Monte Carlo repeats)
why_it_won: "It answers all five sub-questions with one coherent, reusable engine rather than five disconnected models — the same V_E/V_D law and the same CA are re-run with different parameters for breakdown, competition, sensitivity, regional dominance, and biodiversity, which judges read as conceptual unity. The decomposition law is grounded in real literature traits and the parameters are honestly fit (with a shown convergence curve and analytic gradients), so the model looks empirical, not hand-waved. The cellular automaton is the right tool for the prompt's emphasis on spatial hyphal extension and species interaction, and it produces emergent, biologically plausible dynamics (exponential early growth, nutrient-limited decline, ~40-day stabilization, falling-leaf nutrient renewal) that match cited lab observations. Every claim is backed by a specific number and most by a Monte Carlo repeat (e.g., 1000 runs for the 2.89x biodiversity result), giving the rigor judges reward. The Section 3.4.1 'overall analysis' flowchart (Fig. 5) explicitly threads fungal type -> rate law -> three CA definitions -> models (20)/(22), demonstrating the team understood how their own pieces connect."
section_highlights: "Strong structural moves: (1) A one-page Our Work section that lists the four core models by their equation numbers and maps each of the five questions to its answering subsection (Sec 3.2->Q1, 3.3->Q2, 3.4->Q3/Q4, 3.5->Q5) — judges never lose the thread. (2) Building the decomposition law incrementally — first the univariate extension term (Eq.1), then the moisture term (Eqs.2-3), then unifying them (Eq.4) — so the reader follows the derivation rather than being handed a formula. (3) Three numbered Definitions (Extension probability, Decomposition time, Competitive-extension probability) that package the continuous-to-discrete conversions as reusable named objects, each followed by a short 'validity is justified' paragraph. (4) Fig.5, a dependency flowchart of all parameters and models, used as the spine of the Overall Analysis. (5) Consistent quantified conclusions (every figure paired with a percentage or ratio). (6) A genuinely textbook-style 2-page article (Section 5) listing five concrete ecosystem roles of fungi with real figures like fungi accounting for ~13% of photosynthetic CO2 demand — it reads like a biology textbook, exactly as asked. (7) An honest Strengths/Weaknesses section naming parameter-tuning cost and lack of mechanistic biological explanation."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The decomposition law V_D = T*V_E^(1/2) + lambda*a^M + K is dimensionally and physically loose: T enters as a bare multiplicative factor in raw degrees Celsius (so V_D scales with an arbitrary temperature origin), and V_E already depends on T inside the Arrhenius term, double-counting temperature; the form is curve-fit convenience, not derived mechanism (the team admits 'lack of theoretical explanation')

Fitted K = -64.80 with the T*V_E^(1/2) term means V_D is the small difference of large opposing numbers, and V_D is supposed to lie in [0,1] — numerically fragile and not obviously bounded to a valid rate

The decomposition-time formula T_D = c*122/(V_D*500^2) only stays in a sane day-range because of a hand-tuned correction factor c = 5e6; this fudge factor absorbs unit mismatches and is unexplained, so the absolute time scale is essentially calibrated to look right

Many embedded chart tables are internally inconsistent or corrupted (e.g., the dN/dt panels show fungi counts collapsing to 0 after day 10 while the adjacent N_i panel shows them stable around 4.5e4-6e4; the sensitivity panel ends with values like '2011, 651, 851' that are clearly mis-scaled) — a judge skimming figures could be confused, and it suggests the extracted/plotted data was not fully sanity-checked

Arrhenius branch is non-standard: real Arrhenius gives monotonic rate-vs-temperature; here it is repurposed into a symmetric unimodal peak via the 2*Tmax-T reflection, which is an ad hoc inactivation hack rather than the cited enzyme kinetics

Competition is modeled only as a single global occupancy term (sum of all N_j / grid area), so it cannot distinguish which competitor is present or local spatial contact — all interspecies competition is lumped into one mean-field density factor, and mutualism is excluded by assertion
Assumption 7 (higher humidity always raises extension rate, no upper limit) is biologically false for waterlogged conditions and is adopted purely to simplify M

Heavy reliance on one data source (ref [5]) for all six fungi's traits — if that source's trait definitions differ from the model's usage (e.g., the moisture trade-off M definition), the whole parameter base inherits the mismatch
reusable_patterns: The 'analytic rate law + cellular automaton' two-layer pattern: fit a small closed-form law to literature data, then embed it as the local transition rule of a CA to get spatial/temporal dynamics for free — broadly reusable for any spread/diffusion/competition problem (epidemics, fire, invasive species, corrosion)

Continuous-rate-to-probability conversion via sigmoid(1/rate) for per-step CA infection, and rate-to-dwell-time conversion (T_D ~ const/(rate*area)) for decomposition — a clean recipe whenever a continuous ODE-style rate must drive a discrete agent/CA model
Packaging each continuous-to-discrete bridge as a numbered Definition with a short justification paragraph — makes the modeling auditable and gives the writeup reusable, citable objects

Encoding competition as a global occupancy multiplier (1 - total population/capacity) inside an otherwise per-species rule — a lightweight mean-field competition term that needs no pairwise parameters
Deriving and showing explicit SGD/gradient-descent update equations plus a loss-convergence plot as evidence the parameter fit actually converged — a credibility-builder for any data-fitting step

Question-to-section mapping in the Our Work section and a parameter-dependency flowchart (Fig.5) as the spine of the analysis — strong, low-cost structural devices that demonstrate internal coherence to judges

Reusing one simulation engine across a matrix of real-world scenarios (5 climate regions with real NOAA T/H + lat/long, multiple species combinations, and 1000-run Monte Carlo for the diversity ratio) to answer several sub-questions and to report falsifiable percentage/ratio headline numbers
Grounding an open-ended biology prompt in a single authoritative trait dataset and a fixed roster of named real species with concrete parameters, converting a vague problem into a quantitative one
